speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October the 30th, 1996. The name of the survivor is Mr. Norman Tuckman, previous name Tuchmann. The interview it is Leonie Lachmish, and the interview is taking place in Ashkelon, in Israel, and will be conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Today is October the 30th, 1996. My name is Leonie Lachmish, and I have the honor of interviewing Mr. Norman Tuckman today, whose previous name was Tuchmann. The interview is taking place in Ashkelon, in Israel, and we're going to be speaking in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning. Would you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Norman Tuckman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell both your names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Norman. N-O-R-M-A-N. OnIn Tuckman, T-U-C-K-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tuchmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: T-U-C-H-M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name and middle name, Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hebrew name, Nissan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a first na-- it's the first month. N-I-S-S-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other names-- other first names, middle names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did have a name, Leonard Neidhard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind spelling them as well, please? A

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a lengthy name. L-E-O-N-A-R-D Neidhard, N-E-I-D-H-A-R-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. What's your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17th of November, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell me the town and country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. OK. Can you tell me, was-- did your family originate in Munich or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: They both came from a small town in Poland called Teschen. It's in Galicia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and why did they move to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simply for economic reasons. At that time, it was difficult for my father to find parnassa in Poland, and they moved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The turn of the century. The story goes, my father's father, they all lived in Poland. They all lived in the same town, and I feel the small town, that consisted of approximately 200 people, either 100 or 150 were Tuchmann or Tuchmann [INAUDIBLE]. The whole family springs from there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father's father found it almost impossible to earn living for his family, and he followed his brother to America. This is all only hearsay because we have no factual evidence. My father joined his brother, and the very same night as he arrived, both were murdered. My father and his family were left literally destitute and they moved to Germany and lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was murdered?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's father. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: In America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aha. And your father hadn't gone with him to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The bro-- my grandfather's brother went on ahead, and he wrote back and say, why don't you come out to America? It is much easier for you to earn his-- your living here. And he went out and, so we understand, the very first night he arrived there, they were both murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have no idea who by or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or why?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- this is all you know of the matter?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all we know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. I see. So at the turn of the century, your family moved to Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's occupation was a master tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- was he a success-- was he successful? Can you tell me a little about his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a difference between a tailor and a master tailor. A master tailor was a person who could design suits and dresses, cut the pattern, cut the material, and do the stitching. As such, he was reasonably successful, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So could you please tell me your-- both your parents' names and whether you had any other family living with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Nathan Naftalli, and my mother's name was Regina.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Regina.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also Tuchmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also Tuchmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But distantly-- distantly related.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a-- had a sister. Her name was Fanny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fanny. What year was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in '27. 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you tell me what you remember about the Jewish community of Germany as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community of Munich split into two sections. There were basically what you would call the German Jews, or in Israel we call them the [INAUDIBLE]. And there were the Polish Jews. That was the community I belonged to. We were not the second class citizens, but I would rather say the third class citizens. We lived very much together. We prayed together. We had our own synagogue. There was no such thing as being orthodox. We all were orthodox. There was nothing else for us to do. We grew into this life. The synagogue was part of our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say third class citizens, what do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had very little contact with the German Jewish community. All the people in our synagogue were Jews who originally came from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you used to speak amongst yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all speak German. Everybody spoke German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Including the elder people in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody spoke German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spoke German. What about Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish was spoken amongst my parents and their friends. We did not learn Yiddish, but we acquired a certain sense for Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little about-- a bit about the organizations in Munich, Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community was reasonably well organized. We had a central office that dealt with all Jews of Germany-- German and Polish and Russian and what else. Beyond that, there was a Jewish school, a [INAUDIBLE], all facilities. Jewish sports club. It was well organized.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the size of the Jewish community in Munich at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't. And you said a school. Did you go to a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a Jewish school, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judische Volksschule Munchen. And that means Jewish Elementary School, Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what years you were there from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying-- now trying to relate. I think from 1928, '29 till '36, '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in that school, what sort of subjects did you learn? What was your curriculum, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: General education.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on Jewish matters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably more than an outside school, but by no means exclusively Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: The children were all Jewish, were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The children who were all Jewish. The teachers were all Jewish, as far as I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the relations between Jews and non-Jews in Munich before the rise of Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reasonably good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give any examples?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a building. We were the only Jewish family, and all families around us were non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any incidents of anti-Semitism before the rise of Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not before 1933. Not at all. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home life, rituals, and-- and a typical-- maybe a typical Shabbat or festival in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our home life was a happy one. We were a simple family. We had no great luxuries. It is pretty well the same as it's here. [INAUDIBLE] Shabbat, we all went to synagogue. Perhaps not my mother so much, but certainly my father. We came back and we made Kiddush and we sing-- we sung [INAUDIBLE]. It was no different than it is here. In my later years, I was a choir boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat morning-- in the synagogue. Shabbat morning, everybody went to synagogue. When I see everybody, at least the people that I knew, all went to synagogue?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the foods you used to eat in your home? The foods.

speaker: interviewee
text: The food. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't precisely remember, but the choice wasn't that we have today. One ate [INAUDIBLE]. We always said if you had a lot of-- if you had money, you had more meat in the [INAUDIBLE], and if you had no money, you had [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What about books and other literature? What sort of books do you remember in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly boys' books written for my generation. Westerns and such.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Do you remember your goals when you were a child or a young man, the things you were planning for yourself at a young age?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was extremely interested in communication in radio. And as my parents couldn't afford a radio, I joined a club-- a radio club-- and eventually made one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this club?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what age are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are now talking when I was 12, 13. My greatest wish for my bar mitzvah is to have a radio. And eventually I did have a crystal set, which is unknown to our children today. It was a crystal set where you had to twiddle to get a station, and you listened with earphones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now your bar mitzvah was already after the rise of Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what do-- you knew about the rise of the Nazis, what you remember about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one was most concerned because I was just about to leave school to go into a gymnasium, which was a higher education. And whilst we set our tests, we were rejected, being Jewish. And we returned to our basic school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember hearing things at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: One did hear, because we had a infamous concentration camp near Munich-- it was Dachau-- and anybody who didn't toe the line, Jews and non-Jews, went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, anyone that didn't toe the line?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were other cases. Besides religious, there were political, criminal cases. They were all dispatched to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did a Jew have to-- what time are we talking about, 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are now talking '33, '34, '35. My first experience was in Munich, when a young solicitor-- Jewish solicitor-- known to my parents, was marched through the town with a big placard around his neck-- I'm a Jew, I had committed something, something. And he was promptly sent to Dachau, and returned very soon after in a leaded coffin. But at that time, anybody killed in a concentration camp was returned to the Jewish community for burial in Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did others return from the concentration camps alive? At that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, some returned alive, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know-- do you know what they told about there experiences there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what was going on in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember life became-- becoming very uncertain and very unsettled, and my parents made every strenuous effort to emigrate. But nobody would have us. So eventually we did obtain an affidavit to go to United States. And we prepared for immigration. That was now going on for '30-- 1937. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Suitcases were packed, everything was ready for immigration. And our cases were dispatched to be collected in the port of Antwerp, Holland. And there they remained.

speaker: interviewer
text: Antwerp in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Antwerp in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was dispatched for emigration to America. We had obtained our tickets. We had our affidavits. We were just waiting for our number. We were in numbers. The Americans would accept so many per year, and we were waiting for our number to come up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: War started before our number came up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your cases had been sent in 1937?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our cases had been sent in advance. Everything was in Antwerp port-- in a free port-- ready to go. When I say ready to go, don't imagine that we had 50 large cases. No, no. We had perhaps three or four cases. A sewing machine, little bit of furniture, clothing, and whatever my father needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said before that no country was prepared to accept you, no-- no other pla-- no one-- no one wanted you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody wanted us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where your parents tried?

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried everywhere. They tried Cuba, they tried China, they tried everywhere. Jews were then anxious to leave, to leave the best-- wherever they could. Nobody wanted us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you go about trying? Where would one apply?

speaker: interviewee
text: One did hear rumors and the minute you-- you-- you had some information, you went to the consulate. People often sat outside the consulate all night waiting for the consulate to open to find information, to find somewhere to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And back to the-- the matter of the change in the political environment in Germany. You said that you had good relations with your non-Jewish neighbors. Did any of this change after 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain?

speaker: interviewee
text: It became-- became rather distant, not in a-- an enemy-- not a-- not a-- not a hostile feeling towards us, but we had very little contact with the German community?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it happen? How did you feel the change?

speaker: interviewee
text: To give you an example, on the same landing where we lived, the family opposite us, he was a great stamp collector, and so was I. Great stamp collector. I had about 200 stamps, but he had 10,000. And so I spent many a day and evening with him, he explaining his stamp collection and I learned a great lot. After '33 or '34, this contact stopped. I used to see him and say good morning and good afternoon, and that was the end. And that applied to everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with your father and his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much, because my father was in partnership with a non-Jew. The business was in a non-Jewish name. My father handled many more Jews who were emigrating who came to buy suits and have suits made. But beyond that, little changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the relation between him and his partner, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't change at all. He was an extremely honest, decent German citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in touch with him today?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remain in touch with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. No, not at all. Not at all. I don't know-- first of all, he was an old man then, and I-- I've no contact with anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Multsera. Frants Multsera.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was from Munich as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So you said that in 1937, your cases went to Antwerp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And between 1937--

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps it was already getting on for '38, but between '37 and '38, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain about the progression of events between '33 and 1939 from your point of view?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my point of view, from-- first of all, we were not accepted in a non-Jewish school. We returned to our school, but basically the German sch-- the Jewish school that I went to only had four classes-- four elementary classes in Germany. Now, the Jewish community in Munich was confronted with a problem, that the children could not be accepted, nor the teachers. And so they founded four additional classes. That was easier said than done. First of all, we had to find premises adjoining to the previous school. We had makeshift classes, but a fairly good education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of all this when you say they found--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a particular organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish Gemeinde Community.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your bar mitzvah? That took place after the rise of Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell us about the day of your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. There was no such thing as a big bar mitzvah party. We had-- but we-- my bar mitzvah took place traditionally. The party was held at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the atmosphere in the party itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friendly, but tense.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the tension.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the tension.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you think things are going to turn out at that young age?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't think how things were going to turn out. We only thought how soon could we leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what eventually did happen? After all, you didn't-- your family didn't get the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Possibility to go to America. So-- but you did get out in time. Can you please explain what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next incident happened in Germany. There was a great shock to us all when all Polish Jews were collected to be deported. Germany felt that Polish Jews had no place in Germany, no matter how long they lived there. And from one day to the next, all Polish Jews were collected to be deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fortunately-- in 19-- perhaps '38. Fortunately my family and the Polish console were great friends. The Polish console often came to us, and when it happened, we immediately left home and went to the consulate. And there we hid. I don't remember if it was for a day or two days. But all Polish Jews had been collected and had been dispatched to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a date?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not-- we did not leave Munich. We were not dispatched. We were most fortunate. But the Polish Jews who left had a very hard time because Poland would not accept them. The train stood in no man's land for days. There was no water, no sanitation, and then they returned to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you in the consulate. Where did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- I have little recollection, but we were only there a day or two. We returned home and from then onwards, our life continued as semi-normal as it could be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came the Kristallnacht in 1938. Well, that was reasonably, I would say, the end of organized Jewish life as such in Germany. All the synagogues were burned except-- one in a few exceptions-- ours. Ours was not touched for a simple reason. There was-- it was built behind another block of flats, and had they dynamited our synagogue, the building in front would have gone up in flames as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they left our synagogue untouched, and used it as a furniture store. Of course it was ransacked. Everything was plundered. The scrolls-- the sacred Torahs-- everything was burned. But the synagogue as such was untouched.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, many years later-- many years later-- on one of our journeys to Italy, I said to my wife, I'd like to go just to Munich, and we went. We went to Munich to spend just one day there to go to synagogue and to go to the cemetery. There was my seat where we sat, exactly as I'd left it. I could have left my [INAUDIBLE] in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I doubt it. And the-- and the synagogue was a synagogue again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Functioning again, large community, with a German police officer standing outside guarding it with a [INAUDIBLE] gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kristallnacht. What do you remember about Kristallnacht? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Kristallnacht happened-- we were at home. But it happened very quickly in ours. Munich was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] awakened to fire engines, police cars racing about. But there was nothing we could possibly do. The large synagogues were dynamited and burned and plundered, and many, many Jews were rounded up and taken to concentration camps. Not my father. That was the end of '38. On the fifth of January, 1939, only a few days before, my parents decided that I should better go. There was no such thing as preparing. There wasn't much to prepare because we didn't have much. So a case was packed for me. I was sent by train on Kindertransport, and I came to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to take you back one more minute to Kristallnacht. How did you-- you heard the noise from outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard the noise, we heard the fire engines, but not much more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to town and we could see everything was ablaze.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was what made your--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The decision-- your parents' decision.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the end. Jewish shops were plundered and destroy. The whole Jewish community was literally down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a feeling of surprise in your house that such a thing happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Definitely. Great surprise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the decision was made to send you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To England. How did your parents go about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents made this decision and told me that they'd decided it was best for me to leave before we were going to America, because we still planned to go to America. They considered my sister to be too young, and so--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was how old then?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was five years younger, so she was 11, 12. I came on my own with another group of children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you apply to go on the Kindertransport? Do you remember the process?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I've no-- absolutely no idea of. The-- my mother was very active in the community and that all happened within days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: My ca-- my mother was very active in the Jewish community, yes. Very active.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say what sort of things your mother did?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was active with the immigration of children to Israel-- to Palestine at the time-- and also to receive people coming back from concentration camp. They had a community of women who stid-- stood at the platform to receive the men coming out, to give them food, to give them hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Thank you. We'll just take a break here.
